title: Fitting a door frame to a stud wall opening
subtitle: Install a door lining set into a new or existing partition opening
excerpt: How to fit a softwood door lining (frame) into a stud partition wall opening. Covers setting the lining plumb and level, wedging it in position, fixing through the plasterboard, and checking the reveal is consistent around all three sides.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 8213: Windows, doors, and rooflights. City and Guilds Level 2 Carpentry. Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989).

glossaryTerms.definition: The timber frame set into a door opening, consisting of two vertical jambs and a horizontal head piece, that forms the finished surround for the door. The lining is fixed to the wall opening and provides the surface onto which door stops, architrave mouldings, and hinges are fixed. Standard door linings are sold as a set sized to the wall thickness — the lining must cover the full thickness of the wall plus the plaster on each face.

Thin timber wedges or off-cut shims inserted between the door lining and the stud framing to bring the lining to plumb. Always pack at hinge positions so the fixings pass through solid material. Packing behind fixings means the screw or nail does not simply pull the lining sideways as it enters the stud.

content.text: is the three-piece timber set that lines the opening in the stud wall: two jambs (vertical sides) and a head (top horizontal). Once fixed and plumb, it provides the surface for door stops, hinges, and architrave. A lining that is out of plumb gives a door that swings open or closed on its own; a lining that is racked twists the door against the stops.
Door lining set (sized to wall thickness; state the wall thickness when ordering, including both plasterboard faces). 75 mm or 100 mm oval nails or screws.
wedges (timber off-cuts or proprietary plastic packs). A spirit level. A tape measure. A claw hammer or cordless drill. A combination square for squareness checks.

The stud wall opening should be 10 to 15 mm wider and 10 mm taller than the door plus lining width (the lining adds approximately 25 mm to each jamb side). Check the opening for square and plumb using the spirit level. If the head of the opening is not level, pack the low side with timber shims nailed to the trimmer. A stud partition built recently may settle slightly; allow 24 hours after completion before fitting the lining.
Step 2, fix the head piece first

Offer the head piece up to the top of the opening and check it for level. Pack above it with timber wedges until it is level, then nail or screw through the head piece into the header stud above. Check that the head piece face is flush with both faces of the stud wall (before plaster) or centred on the opening (after plaster).

Offer one jamb up to the side of the opening. Hold the spirit level against the face of the jamb and check it is plumb. Slide packing wedges between the jamb and the trimmer stud at each fixing position until the jamb reads plumb in both directions. Fix through the jamb at three points: top, middle, and bottom; always driving the nail or screw through both the lining and the packing wedge into the stud behind. Repeat for the second jamb. Cross-check: measure the diagonal from top corner to bottom opposite corner on both sides; equal diagonals means the frame is square.
Step 4, check the reveal and trim the horns

The reveal (the visible face of the lining in the opening depth) should be consistent all the way around. The lining should project equally from both faces of the stud wall, or flush to one face if the opening is to be plastered on one side. Once fixed and confirmed plumb and square, saw off the horns (the projecting timber tails at the bottom of each jamb that were used as temporary grounds) flush with the floor. The opening is ready for door hanging.

attrs.symptom: Door swings open or closed on its own after hanging
attrs.cause: Jamb is not plumb; the hinge side leans backward or forward.
attrs.fix: Remove the hinge-side jamb fixings, adjust the packing to bring the jamb to true plumb, and re-fix.

attrs.symptom: Door binds against the lining at top or bottom on one side
attrs.cause: Frame is racked — jambs are not parallel to each other.
attrs.fix: Check the diagonal measurements again. A racked lining needs its out-of-plumb jamb re-packed and re-fixed.
